author: @DanParnell-jq4mz
text: Of course the other point about wind mills is that the miller used the mills when the weather allowed it, probably late nights and over the weekends. Perhaps as a society if we had a (slightly) lower expectation of instant power for everything then we could better balance electrical consumption to the weather. This might be as simple as saving our laundry for a dry (sunny? Windy?) day. This would result in less fossil fuel demand when the weather does not suit as well as reduced curtailment from renewables when the electrical demand is less than we are able to consume.

replies.author: @f.d.6667
replies.text:
As an industrial designer, I like their looks too. But as a human factors guy, I also do government-level committee work (pro bono, in a EU country) - mostly in the context of low-frequency noise and vibration. Wind turbine emissions are falling into that category: severe health effects on humans as a result from LFN are known since the mid-60s, WTs as a source of such effects since the early 80s. The mechanisms that are triggering mostly cardio-vascular and vegetative stress reactions in humans are pretty well understood too. Repeated night-time exposure (even below the threshold of conscious hearing) is worst for humans, increasing the risk of Takotsubo-episodes (can trigger a cardiac arrest) and of systemic hypertension (acoustic triggering of the vestibular nuclei, resulting in constant vasoconstriction = "cold" extremities & high blood pressure). Long-term exposure to LFN will result in abnormal thickening of the pericard, elevated morning cortisol (triggering what we call "Pseudo-Cushing's-Syndrome") and abnormal brain function (mostly in the hippocampal region).

Ok - now for the fun part: 1 metric ton of rare earth magnets in a standard wind turbine equals 1 metric ton of finely dispersed uranium and thorium near the mining/processing facilities. Yes, the tails are simply dumped in open bogs and that's it! Cancer rates in the nearby population are around 40% now, if not higher. So yeah, every wind turbine here is killing people in Northern China, Mongolia and Northern India in a horrible way. Not in theory - in reality. The contamination has recently reached the Huang He - which is why some international experts were invited to have a peak at this ongoing nuclear disaster about 5 years ago... Nice to look at from a distance but in their current form a totally "human-incompatible" technology. I wish it was different - but that's where we are today.

author: @sinenomine9093
text:
Here in NYS, we have a 15' x 40' solar panel on my barn & a "net meter" arrangement with the power company. The "net meter" arrangement just means I give them my excess power when it is produced during the daylight and they give me back power whenever I need it, namely at night & in the winter.
  It turns out, the power company's peak demand comes during midday in the hottest months of the summer while my demand is during the winter months when they have excess hydropower. This way they get to avoid buying very expensive "peaker" power (aka brownouts).
   Another advantage to us, our cost of power is fixed at the solar panel installation cost, namely at 5 cents per kWh US for the next 25 years. After 3 years, we have accumulated more than enough solar power credits to power our house, farm & electric vehicle.

replies.author: @PhilR0gers
replies.text:
The louvres are there to regulate the speed.  If the wind is too strong, the mill would run too fast, so opening the louvres slows it down.
Modern wind turbines also adjust their blades when the wind is too high.

author: @f.d.6667
text: A nice illustration of how "green" wind turbines are: 1 metric ton of rare earth magnets in a standard wind turbine equals 1 metric ton of finely dispersed uranium and thorium near the mining/processing facilities. Cancer rates in the nearby population are around 40% now, if not higher. So yeah, every wind turbine here is killing people in Northern China, Mongolia and Northern India. Not in theory - in reality. The contamination has recently reached the Huang He (Yellow River) so officials made reporting on the issue a bit harder. ... Should we talk about the decommissioning process for used rotor blades? There isn't a good one. They end up in landfills ... once the core temp of the dump exceeds 65°C, the epoxy starts to break down and release the bisphenols (around 45%) into the environment. ... 10million tonnes of blades over the next two decades is a LOT of bisphenols. Bye-bye wiener, bye-bye babies. Yup, large-scale wind power clearly is the way forward...

author: @ewjorgy
text:
The opposition to wind turbines is a bit more complex than your assertion that people simply don't like looking at them. Large wind turbines are extremely loud and generate continuous vibrations in the ground that literally destroy the ecosystems in which they are placed. I was the first person in California to install small wind turbines on my house that were connected to the grid in a suburban environment and I can tell you it was an abject failure because of the noise and vibrations. And before any haters try to pretend that I somehow didn't install them properly... I am an engineering physicist that owned my own renewable energy company at that time that specialized in solar, wind, hydro, etc., and I worked with the experts at one of the largest wind turbine manufacturers at that time to construct a tower system designed to damp out the resonant frequencies of this turbine system and it still was untenable in practice. The Altamont wind farm just a few miles from where I grew up was the first large commercial wind farm and it demonstrated how  the entire ecosystem for miles around was effectively obliterated by these same issues. The wind farms were actually forced to buy out people's homes that were rendered unlivable due to the proximity of the turbines which in some cases were miles away.  The only thing that can survive around wind turbines is livestock because they are not given a choice.
That said, I still believe wind power has its place but there are many complex issues that have to be dealt with before you can just throw a turbine up. Its not simply  esthetics. There are always tradeoffs.

author: @AmandaBrecc
text: On a different note. Love your stuff. Transport. Magnets n coils. What if you made a disc like the ones thrown in that sport. Imbed the magnets in the disc, slot the disc in a track and knowing the disc diameter you know the linear distance of the disc in the track. One could then theoretically, power a coil with as much energy as you like to force the disc to spin which would propel the disc down the track and knowing where the disc will be, another coil could be fired at that point to propel the disc and so forth and so on. Then what if one were to have a sled with three to five on either side. One could hit the magnet perfectly like a baseball batter and how long could the track be? Then what if one were to make two sets. This gets rather interesting as one could get the disc up to speed before making contact with the track? I could be mistaken.

text:
Yes, we will need a lot more electrical infrastructure to replace carbon-based fuels, both from the production to the distribution.
Yes, many wind turbines aren't pretty.
Personally, though, I think it's a better option than continuing to suck on the Saudi or Soviet Oil "Teats" for the rest of our history.

author: @gregcooper8407

author: @Istandby666
text: When you drive across Western Kansas and you look out on these farm land's. There are big wind turbines all over. Why? Why did these farm's allow this? Because they get paid a lot to have these wind turbines on their property.
